title: The Best Retirement Plans for Self-Employed Individuals

interlinking_suggestions.title: Financial Management for Freelancers: Budgeting, Saving for Taxes, and Retirement
SEP IRA vs. Solo 401(k): Which is Better for You?
How Much Should You Save for Retirement as a Freelancer?

author.name: Ben Carter
author.bio: A certified financial planner specializing in the unique challenges faced by US citizens abroad. Ben's articles provide actionable advice on everything from FBAR and FATCA compliance to retirement planning for expats.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Prioritize the Solo 401(k) over a SEP IRA to gain superior control, including a Roth option for tax diversification and access to participant loans for strategic capital.
Critically, verify you have U.S.-taxable 'earned income' before contributing from abroad, as using the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) can reduce your legal contribution limit to zero.
Automate your savings by creating a rule to transfer a fixed percentage of every client payment into a separate holding account, ensuring contributions are systematic, not an afterthought.
Integrate a Health Savings Account (HSA) if eligible, treating it as a 'stealth IRA' that offers a triple tax advantage for medical expenses and long-term growth.
summary: Self-employed professionals often fail to build a robust financial system, missing key opportunities for tax efficiency and control. The core advice is to act as the CEO of your "Business-of-One" by choosing a Solo 401(k) for its superior flexibility and loan features, integrating a triple-tax-advantaged HSA, and automating contributions. By implementing this strategic framework, you gain executive control over your capital, creating a powerful financial engine that systematically converts current income into long-term freedom.
ai_overview_answer: For a self-employed professional, the optimal strategy is to establish a Solo 401(k) as the core of their personal treasury and benefits department. The Solo 401(k) is superior to a SEP IRA because it allows for a Roth contribution component, providing critical tax diversification, and features a dual employee/employer contribution structure for greater savings flexibility. This plan also uniquely offers a participant loan feature, creating a strategic, self-funded credit line of up to $50,000. To build a comprehensive benefits package, integrate a Health Savings Account (HSA) alongside the Solo 401(k) to leverage its triple-tax advantage for medical and retirement savings. For US expats, it is crucial to understand that income excluded via the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) does not count as compensation for retirement contributions. Finally, systematize your savings by creating an automated rule to transfer a fixed percentage of every payment into a separate holding account. This framework provides maximum control, tax efficiency, and strategic financial options for your "business-of-one."
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

itemListElement.name: The Best Retirement Plans for Self-Employed Individuals

@graph.headline: The Best Retirement Plans for Self-Employed Individuals
@graph.description: Explore the top retirement plans for self-employed pros. Our CEO's guide compares the Solo 401(k) vs. SEP IRA on control, loans, and Roth options. Take control today.

step.name: Select Your Treasury's Core Engine: Solo 401(k) vs. SEP IRA
step.text: Your first executive action is to select the primary engine for your personal treasury. This choice between the two main retirement plans for self-employed professionals—the Solo 401(k) and the SEP IRA—is not about contribution limits alone. It is about defining the scope of your control over your capital and tax strategy. For the high-income sole proprietor, this decision should be framed around a single word: control. While both plans boast high contribution limits (up to $69,000 for 2024), the Solo 401(k) is engineered for a far greater degree of executive authority. Its most significant advantage is the ability to include a Roth contribution component. This feature is a strategic game-changer, allowing you to make post-tax contributions that grow and can be withdrawn in retirement completely tax-free. This provides critical tax diversification—a level of financial control a SEP IRA simply cannot offer. Furthermore, the structural design of the Solo 401(k) recognizes your dual role as both "employee" and "employer," allowing you to contribute in both capacities: As the "employee," you can contribute up to 100% of your compensation, capped at $23,000 for 2024. As the "employer," you can contribute an additional amount, generally up to 25% of your net adjusted self-employment income. This dual structure provides immense flexibility. The direct dollar-for-dollar employee contribution often allows you to save more at lower income levels compared to a SEP IRA, which is funded exclusively by the employer contribution. For a professional with variable cash flow, this is a crucial advantage. The SEP IRA remains a simpler, but less versatile, alternative. Its primary advantage is its ease of setup, making it a viable option for those establishing a plan at the last minute. For the forward-thinking CEO, however, the negligible extra setup time for a Solo 401(k) is a small price to pay for a lifetime of enhanced control.

step.name: Architect Your Corporate Benefits Package
step.text: With your treasury's engine selected, the next step is to build out a sophisticated benefits package. A true CEO doesn't just plan for the distant future; they build a comprehensive system that provides strategic advantages and a robust safety net today. Unique to the Solo 401(k), this feature allows you to borrow the lesser of $50,000 or 50% of your vested account value without tax or penalty. This is not a desperate raid on your retirement savings; it is a strategic capital maneuver. Think of it as your business's internal treasury providing a low-interest loan to its CEO. The interest you pay goes directly back into your own retirement account. This creates a private credit line for scenarios like: Covering a sudden tax liability without selling investments. Funding a business opportunity, such as new equipment or a high-value certification. Bridging a cash-flow gap when a major client pays late. The loan must generally be repaid within five years, ensuring the integrity of your long-term investment strategy. If you have a high-deductible health plan (HDHP), the Health Savings Account (HSA) is arguably the most powerful savings tool in the tax code. It is a secondary retirement vehicle that doubles as your healthcare safety net, offering a rare triple tax advantage: Contributions are tax-deductible. Funds can be invested and grow tax-free. Withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free. For 2024, you can contribute up to $4,150 for self-only coverage or $8,300 for family coverage. Unlike an FSA, your HSA balance rolls over and compounds annually. After age 65, you can withdraw funds for any reason (taxed as regular income), while withdrawals for medical expenses remain tax-free forever. For the highest-earning sole proprietors, a Defined Benefit Plan functions like a traditional pension. These plans are complex and require actuarial calculations, but they allow for massive, potentially six-figure, tax-deductible contributions that far exceed 401(k) limits. Acknowledging this option is a critical part of long-term strategic planning for when your "Business-of-One" is operating at the highest level.

step.name: The Global Professional's Compliance Desk
step.text: For the global professional, operating your "Business-of-One" from abroad introduces a layer of compliance that demands a specific, strategic response. Get this wrong, and your best-laid plans can unravel. The most significant compliance trap for Americans abroad is the interaction between the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) and retirement contributions. The FEIE allows you to exclude a substantial portion of your income from U.S. taxes ($126,500 for 2024). However, here is the essential truth: Income you exclude using the FEIE does not count as "compensation" for the purposes of IRA or Solo 401(k) contributions. If you earn $120,000 abroad and exclude all of it with the FEIE, you have also reduced your eligible compensation for retirement contributions to zero. For that year, your legal contribution limit is $0. Your ability to contribute to a U.S. retirement plan from abroad requires you to weigh your immediate tax burden against your long-term goals. Choosing the FTC over the FEIE is a conscious trade-off. In a high-tax country, the FTC may eliminate your U.S. tax liability while preserving your ability to save. In a zero-tax country, this choice means willingly paying some U.S. tax to unlock the far greater benefit of tax-deferred growth. Finally, your Solo 401(k) must be established by a U.S.-based business entity (e.g., a U.S. LLC). Even if you operate globally, your business—for retirement plan purposes—must exist under U.S. law. This is a non-negotiable pillar of a compliant global retirement strategy.

step.name: The CFO Mandate: Systematize Your Contributions
step.text: A CEO builds systems to ensure critical functions happen automatically. Your retirement funding should be a smooth, automated process—not a frantic scramble before a tax deadline. Adopt a "Profit First" mentality: your retirement contribution is the first and most critical bill your business pays. Here is the system to make this a reality: Establish a "Future You" Holding Account. Open a separate business savings account at a different bank from your primary checking. This account should have no debit card and no easy transfer links. Its sole purpose is to hold funds earmarked for retirement, creating a powerful psychological and financial separation. Create an "Invoice Paid" Automation Rule. In your business bank account, create an automated rule: for every client payment that arrives, transfer a fixed percentage—a non-negotiable 15%, 20%, or 25%—directly into your "Future You" holding account. This turns variable income into a consistent savings habit. Execute Your Contribution with Confidence. With funds consistently accumulating, making your contribution becomes a simple, stress-free administrative task. Whether you transfer funds quarterly or annually, the cash is already set aside. There is no year-end panic. You are not hoping to save; you are executing a pre-determined financial plan.

mainEntity.name: Which is better for a high-income consultant, a Solo 401(k) or a SEP IRA?

acceptedAnswer.text: For a high-income consultant with no employees (other than a spouse), the Solo 401(k) is almost always the superior vehicle. Its dual-contribution structure often allows you to save more, faster. Crucially, it offers a Roth option for tax diversification, a feature the SEP IRA lacks.

mainEntity.name: What is the deadline to open a Solo 401(k) for 2024?

acceptedAnswer.text: To make "employee" contributions (up to $23,000) for the 2024 tax year, the plan must be established by December 31, 2024. However, you can establish the plan up until your business tax filing deadline in 2025 and still make "employer" profit-sharing contributions for the 2024 tax year. Missing the December 31 deadline means forfeiting the significant employee contribution for that year.

mainEntity.name: How do I calculate my maximum Solo 401(k) contribution?

acceptedAnswer.text:
Your total maximum contribution is the sum of your employee and employer contributions, which cannot exceed $69,000 for 2024.

*   **Employee Contribution:** 100% of your compensation up to **$23,000 for 2024** (plus a $7,500 catch-up contribution if you are age 50 or over).
*   **Employer Contribution:** Up to 25% of your net adjusted self-employment income. This calculation is nuanced (it's based on your net earnings after deducting one-half of your self-employment taxes). Using an online calculator from a plan provider or consulting a tax professional is the most reliable approach.

mainEntity.name: Can a US expat open a Solo 401(k)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. A U.S. citizen operating a business abroad can open and contribute to a Solo 401(k), provided their business is registered in the U.S. and they have eligible U.S.-taxable compensation.

mainEntity.name: Does the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) affect my retirement contributions?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, critically. Any income you exclude from U.S. taxes using the FEIE **cannot** be used to justify contributions to a retirement account. If you earn $120,000 and exclude it all, your eligible compensation for contributions is $0 for that year. You must have earned income *above* the exclusion amount or use the Foreign Tax Credit instead to be able to contribute.

mainEntity.name: Are there specific retirement plans for digital nomads with US citizenship?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. A U.S. citizen uses the same powerful tools available to their domestic counterparts: the Solo 401(k), SEP IRA, etc. The key is not finding a different *plan*, but applying the rules of these existing plans to the unique compliance realities of a global career.

body.content: **The CEO's Playbook: Building the Treasury & Benefits Department for Your "Business-of-One"**

Your retirement plan is not merely a savings account; it is the primary capital-compounding engine of your personal enterprise. The choice you make here has profound, long-term implications for your tax efficiency, investment flexibility, and ultimately, your control. As the CEO of your business, you must make a deliberate executive decision, not follow a template. This playbook outlines the strategic framework for building a financial architecture that serves your ambitions.
**Step 1: Select Your Treasury's Core Engine: Solo 401(k) vs. SEP IRA**

Your first executive action is to select the primary engine for your personal treasury. This choice between the two main retirement plans for self-employed professionals—the Solo 401(k) and the SEP IRA—is not about contribution limits alone. It is about defining the scope of your control over your capital and tax strategy.

For the high-income sole proprietor, this decision should be framed around a single word: **control**. While both plans boast high contribution limits (up to $69,000 for 2024), the Solo 401(k) is engineered for a far greater degree of executive authority. Its most significant advantage is the ability to include a **Roth contribution component**. This feature is a strategic game-changer, allowing you to make post-tax contributions that grow and can be withdrawn in retirement completely tax-free. This provides critical tax diversification—a level of financial control a SEP IRA simply cannot offer.
Furthermore, the structural design of the Solo 401(k) recognizes your dual role as both "employee" and "employer," allowing you to contribute in both capacities:

items: **As the "employee,"** you can contribute up to 100% of your compensation, capped at $23,000 for 2024.
**As the "employer,"** you can contribute an additional amount, generally up to 25% of your net adjusted self-employment income.

body.content: This dual structure provides immense flexibility. The direct dollar-for-dollar employee contribution often allows you to save more at lower income levels compared to a SEP IRA, which is funded exclusively by the employer contribution. For a professional with variable cash flow, this is a crucial advantage.

The SEP IRA remains a simpler, but less versatile, alternative. Its primary advantage is its ease of setup, making it a viable option for those establishing a plan at the last minute. For the forward-thinking CEO, however, the negligible extra setup time for a Solo 401(k) is a small price to pay for a lifetime of enhanced control.
**Step 2: Architect Your Corporate Benefits Package**

With your treasury's engine selected, the next step is to build out a sophisticated benefits package. A true CEO doesn't just plan for the distant future; they build a comprehensive system that provides strategic advantages and a robust safety net today.
**Leverage the Solo 401(k) Participant Loan as a Strategic Capital Source**

Unique to the Solo 401(k), this feature allows you to borrow the lesser of **$50,000 or 50% of your vested account value** without tax or penalty. This is not a desperate raid on your retirement savings; it is a strategic capital maneuver. Think of it as your business's internal treasury providing a low-interest loan to its CEO. The interest you pay goes directly back into your own retirement account. This creates a private credit line for scenarios like:

items: **Covering a sudden tax liability** without selling investments.
**Funding a business opportunity**, such as new equipment or a high-value certification.
**Bridging a cash-flow gap** when a major client pays late.

body.content: The loan must generally be repaid within five years, ensuring the integrity of your long-term investment strategy.
**Integrate a Health Savings Account (HSA) as a "Stealth IRA"**

If you have a high-deductible health plan (HDHP), the Health Savings Account (HSA) is arguably the most powerful savings tool in the tax code. It is a secondary retirement vehicle that doubles as your healthcare safety net, offering a rare **triple tax advantage**:

items: Funds can be invested and grow tax-free.
Withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free.

body.content: For 2024, you can contribute up to **$4,150 for self-only coverage** or **$8,300 for family coverage**. Unlike an FSA, your HSA balance rolls over and compounds annually. After age 65, you can withdraw funds for any reason (taxed as regular income), while withdrawals for medical expenses remain tax-free forever.
**Acknowledge the Next Level: The Defined Benefit Plan**

For the highest-earning sole proprietors, a Defined Benefit Plan functions like a traditional pension. These plans are complex and require actuarial calculations, but they allow for massive, potentially six-figure, tax-deductible contributions that far exceed 401(k) limits. Acknowledging this option is a critical part of long-term strategic planning for when your "Business-of-One" is operating at the highest level.
**Step 3: The Global Professional's Compliance Desk**

For the global professional, operating your "Business-of-One" from abroad introduces a layer of compliance that demands a specific, strategic response. Get this wrong, and your best-laid plans can unravel.
**The FEIE Landmine: Understand "Earned Income"**

The most significant compliance trap for Americans abroad is the interaction between the **Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE)** and retirement contributions. The FEIE allows you to exclude a substantial portion of your income from U.S. taxes ($126,500 for 2024). However, here is the essential truth: **Income you exclude using the FEIE does *not* count as "compensation" for the purposes of IRA or Solo 401(k) contributions.**

If you earn $120,000 abroad and exclude all of it with the FEIE, you have also reduced your eligible compensation for retirement contributions to zero. For that year, your legal contribution limit is $0.
**A Clear Framework for Expat Contributions**
Your ability to contribute to a U.S. retirement plan from abroad requires you to weigh your immediate tax burden against your long-term goals.

rows: You can make contributions based *only* on the income you earn *above* the exclusion amount. If you earn $150,000, you can contribute based on the $23,500 that remains taxable.

To contribute, you must forgo the FEIE. Instead, use the FTC, which gives you a dollar-for-dollar credit for income taxes paid to a foreign government. This keeps your income "earned" in the eyes of the IRS.

body.content: Choosing the FTC over the FEIE is a conscious trade-off. In a high-tax country, the FTC may eliminate your U.S. tax liability while preserving your ability to save. In a zero-tax country, this choice means willingly paying some U.S. tax to unlock the far greater benefit of tax-deferred growth.
**The "Location of Business" Test**

Finally, your Solo 401(k) must be established by a U.S.-based business entity (e.g., a U.S. LLC). Even if you operate globally, your business—for retirement plan purposes—must exist under U.S. law. This is a non-negotiable pillar of a compliant global retirement strategy.
**Step 4: The CFO Mandate: Systematize Your Contributions**

A CEO builds systems to ensure critical functions happen automatically. Your retirement funding should be a smooth, automated process—not a frantic scramble before a tax deadline. Adopt a **"Profit First" mentality**: your retirement contribution is the first and most critical bill your business pays.
Here is the system to make this a reality:

items: **Establish a "Future You" Holding Account.** Open a separate business savings account at a different bank from your primary checking. This account should have no debit card and no easy transfer links. Its sole purpose is to hold funds earmarked for retirement, creating a powerful psychological and financial separation.

**Create an "Invoice Paid" Automation Rule.** In your business bank account, create an automated rule: for every client payment that arrives, transfer a fixed percentage—a non-negotiable 15%, 20%, or 25%—directly into your "Future You" holding account. This turns variable income into a consistent savings habit.

**Execute Your Contribution with Confidence.** With funds consistently accumulating, making your contribution becomes a simple, stress-free administrative task. Whether you transfer funds quarterly or annually, the cash is already set aside. There is no year-end panic. You are not hoping to save; you are executing a pre-determined financial plan.

body.content: **Frequently Asked Questions**
Think of this section as your direct line to corporate counsel. Here, we address the most common technical questions that arise once your strategic framework is in place.
**Which is better for a high-income consultant, a Solo 401(k) or a SEP IRA?**

For a high-income consultant with no employees (other than a spouse), the Solo 401(k) is almost always the superior vehicle. Its dual-contribution structure often allows you to save more, faster. Crucially, it offers a Roth option for tax diversification, a feature the SEP IRA lacks.

rows: Maximizing control, tax flexibility, and strategic options.

body.content: **What is the deadline to open a Solo 401(k) for 2024?**

To make "employee" contributions (up to $23,000) for the 2024 tax year, the plan must be established by December 31, 2024. However, you can establish the plan up until your business tax filing deadline in 2025 and still make "employer" profit-sharing contributions for the 2024 tax year. Missing the December 31 deadline means forfeiting the significant employee contribution for that year.
**How do I calculate my maximum Solo 401(k) contribution?**
Your total maximum contribution is the sum of your employee and employer contributions, which cannot exceed $69,000 for 2024.

items: **Employee Contribution:** 100% of your compensation up to **$23,000 for 2024** (plus a $7,500 catch-up contribution if you are age 50 or over).

**Employer Contribution:** Up to 25% of your net adjusted self-employment income. This calculation is nuanced (it's based on your net earnings after deducting one-half of your self-employment taxes). Using an online calculator from a plan provider or consulting a tax professional is the most reliable approach.

body.content: **Can a US expat open a Solo 401(k)?**
Yes. A U.S. citizen operating a business abroad can open and contribute to a Solo 401(k), provided their business is registered in the U.S. and they have eligible U.S.-taxable compensation.
**Does the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) affect my retirement contributions?**

Yes, critically. Any income you exclude from U.S. taxes using the FEIE **cannot** be used to justify contributions to a retirement account. If you earn $120,000 and exclude it all, your eligible compensation for contributions is $0 for that year. You must have earned income *above* the exclusion amount or use the Foreign Tax Credit instead to be able to contribute.
**Are there specific retirement plans for digital nomads with US citizenship?**

No. A U.S. citizen uses the same powerful tools available to their domestic counterparts: the Solo 401(k), SEP IRA, etc. The key is not finding a different *plan*, but applying the rules of these existing plans to the unique compliance realities of a global career.
**Conclusion: You Are the CEO—Take Control**

Choosing your retirement plan is not a passive item on a to-do list; it is an executive action. It is the moment you decide to stop operating like a freelancer and start leading like the CEO of a durable enterprise: your career.

By framing this process as the creation of your own Treasury and Benefits department, you move from a place of anxiety to a position of power. An employee waits for HR to hand them a pamphlet of options. A founder designs the entire benefits structure from the ground up to serve the long-term prosperity of the enterprise and its key stakeholder—you.

A Solo 401(k) with a loan provision is your self-funded credit line. An HSA is a triple-tax-advantaged investment vehicle. A well-chosen, systematically funded retirement plan is the quiet engine that converts today's revenue into tomorrow's freedom. The act of automating your contributions is a testament to your strategic control. This is the final and most important project you will ever lead. Take control. Build your treasury. Secure your future.
